metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: preparation of plan of action for conducting thorough investigaion of the case

page_content: in order to achieve any goal, a proper plan should be devised and implemented properly. crime investigation is an intricate subject and the investigation conducted by the i.o.s is scrutinized by the courts as well as defense counsels minutely. any flaw or defect in investigation will lead to acquittal of the cases. the investigating officers, due to their busy schedule of work, may miss to collect certain crucial evidence to prove the offence against the accused. in order to overcome the above problems, soon after issuing fir, the i.o. should prepare a detailed plan of action to conduct exhaustive investigation in a systematic manner and collect all the evidences to establish the offence. in most of the cases, initially the information for collection of evidence may be minimal; during the investigation many new facts would come to the notice of i.o. regarding involvement of accused persons, etc. therefore the i.o. can add further points in plan of action for collection of further evidence. the i.o. shall collect the evidence which is incorporated in the plan of action for conducting thorough and exhaustive investigation of the case to prove the guilt of the accused beyond reasonable doubt. a model copy of plan of action is enclosed.

metadata.heading: if the complainant comes to police station with injuries

page_content: - take the injured person immediately to hospital for treatment. - prepare the requisition addressing the medical officer to examine the injured person. - take the medical certificate from doctor. - take the complaint from injured/eyewitness/relatives etc for issue of fir. - make endorsement on the complaint and issue fir by applying appropriate sections of law. - visit scene; conduct panchanama before panchas, sieze the material objects which are relevant to crime, draw the rough sketch. - examine and record statements of witnesses. - arrest and collect relevant documents and file charge sheet.

page_content: the facts of the case are that on 02-02-2022 at about 1730 hours received a complaint from smt r roja w/o r keshawanath goud, age: 37 years, occ: house wife, r/o: h.no: 18-9-311, kummarwadi, chandrayangutta, hyderabad stating that she along with her husband stays in above said residence in second floor. on 31-01-2022 she went to hospital and returned back at about 1530 hours, while she was talking to her cousin by name r. rama w/o amarnath age:33 years who stays at same building in the ground floor regarding how to use medicines prescribed by doctor, meantime her brother-in-law's son by name r akshay prasad s/o prabhakar goud, age:25 years resides beside her portion has listened to their conversation and started abusing her in filthy language by believing that they are abusing akshay's family . when she asked him to stop abusing her, he started beating her with his hands on her face, head, and ribs, due to which she fell unconscious. later, her family members shifted her to life line hospital. after treatment she returned home, due to drowsiness she was not able to come to ps immediately. on 01-02-2022 swelling and pain on her nose increased. she came to police station and informed about the incident. the police personnel sent her to owaisi hospital where the doctor treated her under mlc.no1136650/22 and declared that her nasal bone has been fractured. hence she requested to take necessary action.

page_content: as per the version of the witnesses, it is revealed that lw-01 and lw02 are cousins. lw-03 is the daughter of lw-01. the accused akshay prasad is the relative of lw-01 and lw-02. on 31-01-2022 lw-01 had gone to hospital and returned back at about 1530 hours, while she was talking to her cousin lw-02 r. rama who stays at same building in the ground floor regarding how to use medicines prescribed by doctor, in the meantime accused r akshay prasad, intervened into conversation of lw-01 and lw02, and started abusing lw-01 as lanjadaana, dongamunda in filthy language by believing lw-01and lw-02 were abusing accused akshay's family. lw-01 asked accused to stop abusing but the accused all of sudden, started beating lw-01 with his hands on her face, head, and ribs, due to which lw-01 has sustained fracture to nasal bone, right eye discolouration, and other injuries. lw-02 and lw-03 witnessed the incident. lw-6 issued medical certificate. the complainant did not approach to the ps on the day of offence due to hospital treatment. thus the evidence on record well established that the accused person committed an offence punishable u/s 325, 509 ipc /117(1)(2),79 bns.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: in simple the complaint / report must cover the following 5"w"s & 1 "h".

page_content: - 1-w-what happened? - 2- w- when did it happen? - 3- w -where did it happen? - 4- w -who possibly committed the crime? - 5-w-why it happened (motive) - 1- h- how was it committed?

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: salient points to be remembered while registering a fir:

page_content: - a. write the fir immediately with all available details in the report/petition, and enter the brief of facts at once in the gd. - b. name of the complainant/informant, father's/husband's name, age, occupation and residence should be noted. - c. the time of reporting at the police station and brief particulars of the report and the crime number and section of law indicating the action taken. - d. obtain the signature (if illiterate, the thumb impression) of the complainant or informant on all the pages of the statement. - e. record on the original written statement of the informant, the date and hour of its receipt and through whom it was received. - f. ascertain and incorporate in the fir the reasons for the delay, if any, in the receipt of the report or in the making of the complaint at the police station. - g. place of occurrence and distance and direction from the police station. - h. name and address of the accused. (the full name of each accused with aliases, if any, address and father's name) - i. details of property stolen/taken away, if any. if the list of stolen property is lengthy, it must be made on a separate sheet of a paper and attached to the fir and fact mentioned in this column with signatures of the both the complainant and the sho.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 2 (b). acid attack cases: grievous hurt by using acid u/s 326 a ipc/124 bns case

page_content: sec. 326 (a) ipc/124 bns: (gender neutral) voluntarily causing grievous hurt by use of acid etc. (cognizable and non - bailable)

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 2 (b). acid attack cases: grievous hurt by using acid u/s 326 a ipc/124 bns case

page_content: to be investigated by inspector of police under close super vision of sdpo as it is a grave crime.
- i. voluntarily causing grievous hurt by use of acid resulting in burns, disfigurement or disability. - ii. causing permanent or partial damage or deformity.
- i. immediate admission in special burns ward to preclude spreading of infection. send requisition to magistrate for recording dying declaration depending upon percentage of burns.

- ii. as acid is likely to sprinkle on the accused also, while throwing on victim, immediate arrest would link the accused to crime scene. his presence at scene can be ascertained from cell phone tower location or cctv footage (in cities). - iii. oral evidence of the victim. (sec.60 of evidence act/55 bsa). recording evidence through audio-video electronic means u/s 161 (3) cr.pc. photographs of victim to be sent to court with remand cd. - iv. scene should be visited by the investigating officer should be video and photographed, thoroughly examined. - v. collection of material evidence, direct/circumstantial evidence. - vi. arrest the accused after preliminary investigation establishes the offence. - vii. establish mens rea like failed love, hatred, contempt etc, and interrogate the accused. - viii. collection of physical evidence -like acid bottle, controlled earth, owner's statement). - ix. collection of circumstantial evidence/fsl report on ph of acid, type of acid. - x. examination of eye witnesses-people who have seen, who have admitted the person in a nearby hospital etc. - xi. digital evidence like smss, laptop, e-mails and any other social media etc.,
(sec. 65 a evidence act/62 bsa) when the accused is following the victim, obtaining medical report on degree of burns and quantity required to cause such burns.

- xii. as per the sec- 357-c cr.pc / 397 bnss victims of acid attack have to be provided first-aid and medical treatment free of cost by the private/public/state/central govt. hospitals and in turn inform the local police, failure to provide free medical treatment is punishable u/s 166 b ipc with imprisonment for a term up to 1 year our with fine or with both. - xiii. write to collector, pd icds, secretary, dlsa for payment of compensation as per rules/govt. orders. - xiv. enclose all documents in the charge sheet including the photograph of victim before acid attack and after attack as exhibit.
1. shall not be less than ten years, may extend to imprisonment for life, and with fine. fine shall be paid to the victim.
2. trial by court of sessions. sdpo/inspector should be personally present. if necessary conduct audio-video electronic trial u/s 275 cr.pc/310 bnss if victim is immobile.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 2 (c). causing hurt to deter public servant 332 ipc/121 bns

page_content: whoever voluntarily causes hurt to any person being a public servant in the discharge of his duty as such public servant , or with intent to prevent to order that person or any other public servant from discharging his duty as such public servant or in consequence of anything done or attempted to be done by that person in the lawful discharge of his duty as such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 2 (d). assault or criminal force to deter public servant 353 ipc/132 bns.

page_content: whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person being a public servant in the execution of his duty as such public servant or deter that person from discharging his duty as such public servant, or in consequence of anything done or attempted to be done by such person in the lawful discharge of his duty as such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 2 (d). assault or criminal force to deter public servant 353 ipc/132 bns.

page_content: in above both c and d paragraphs follow the sop mentioned in hurt and rioting cases.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 2 (e). investigation of case and counter case (pso-566-2)

page_content: case and counter : -generally in rioting, bodily offence cases there is a case and a counter. in such cases the io faces the problem of determining the aggressor. the oral evidence by both parties will be more or less identical. genesis of the offence, evidence of independent witness, scientific clues available at the scene, the preparation for the offence, the place of offence and the subsequent conduct would provide help in determining the aggressors, where it is difficult to determine as to who is the aggressor and where evidence shows that there is preparation on both sides, the right course of action is to prosecute both on the basis of free fight.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 2 (e). investigation of case and counter case (pso-566-2)

page_content: - an assembly of five or more persons is designated an 'unlawful assembly' if the common object of the persons composing that assembly is-
(first) - to overawe by criminal force, or show of criminal force,
- the central or any state government or - parliament or the legislature of any state, or - any public servant in the exercise of the lawful power of such public servant; or
(second) - to resist the execution of any law, or of any legal process; or
(third) - to commit any mischief or criminal trespass, or other offence; or
(fourth) - by means of criminal force, or

- show of criminal force, - to any person, - to take or obtain possession of any property, or - to deprive any person of the enjoyment of a right of way, or of the use of water or - other incorporeal right of which he is in possession or - enjoyment, or to enforce any right or supposed right; or
(fifth) - by means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to compel any person to do what he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do what he is legally entitled to do.
explanation - an assembly which was not unlawful when it assembled, may subsequently become an unlawful assembly.
section 146 ipc/191(1) bns rioting: - whenever force or violence is used
- by an unlawful assembly or - by any member thereof, - in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, - every member of such assembly is guilty of the offence of rioting.
section 147 ipc/192(2) bns punishment for rioting (this section prescribes the punishment for the offence or rioting under sec.146/191(1) bns). whoever is guilty of rioting
- shall be punished with imprisonment of - either description for a term, which - may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

- a. five or more persons were assembled - b. they constituted an unlawful assembly - c. the members of the unlawful assembly used force or violence - d. the accused was a member of that unlawful assembly - e. in prosecution of its common object, the unlawful assembly used force
the offence is cognizable, bailable and is triable by any magistrate.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 148 ipc/191(3) bns : rioting, armed with deadly weapon :

page_content: whoever is guilty of rioting being armed with a deadly weapon or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 148 ipc/191(3) bns : rioting, armed with deadly weapon :

page_content: extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

- a. five or more persons were assembled - b. they constitute an unlawful assembly - c. the members of such assembly used force or violence - d. the accused was a member of that unlawful assembly - e. in prosecution of the common object the unlawful assembly used force - f. the accused was armed with a deadly weapon or with a weapon of offence likely to cause death
analysis and case law: the offence punishable under this section (148 ipc/191(3) bns) is an aggravated form of rioting under sec.147/191(2) bns.

- this section will be attracted only when a rioter is armed with a deadly weapon or with a weapon of offence likely to cause death. - a person cannot be punished under this section unless he actually had a deadly weapon.
the offence under this section is cognizable, bailable and triable by a magistrate of the 1 st class.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section.149 ipc/190 bns: every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object:

page_content: - if an offence committed by any member of an unlawful assembly - in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or - such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly is guilty of that offence.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section.149 ipc/190 bns: every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object:

page_content: analysis and case law: this section does not create a new offence but deals with the vicarious liability of the members of an unlawful assembly

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section.149 ipc/190 bns: every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object:

page_content: 1. for acts done in furtherance of its common object: and 2. for such offences as its members knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of its common object. 3. this section declares that every member of an unlawful assembly having a common object in mind is responsible for the acts committed by any other member of that assembly in pursuance of such common object or 4. one which he must have known was reasonably likely to be committed in prosecution of its common object. 5. he is guilty of the substantive offence and punishable for that offence. where the offence is not committed in prosecution of its common object, the person who actually committed the offence is liable.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: when complainant/petitioner comes to police station to lodge the complaint

page_content: offer chair to the petitioner and make him comfortable which would repose confidence in him.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: if the complainant brought written complaint/petition-

page_content: - take petition, go through the contents - enquire with him generally about the contents to confirm the facts

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: if the complainant wants to report matter orally-

page_content: - offer him a white paper to write and obtain the same in writing under the signature of the complainant. - if he cannot write the petition, the sho by himself or through his subordinate shall reduce oral statement of complainant into writing, read over the statement to the complainant and take his signature after him admitting the contents are correct.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: if the complainant comes to police station with injuries-

page_content: - take the injured person immediately to hospital for treatment, by providing first aid if necessary. - prepare the requisition addressing the medical officer for - examining the injured person. - provide treatment. - and to provide medical/injury certificate. - take the petition from injured person if he has nominal injuries. - if the victim has serious injuries take the complaint from eye witness for registering a case. - the statement can be taken while shifting injured person to hospital if it is not causing any inconvenience to injured person.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: in the instances mentioned above (a, b,& c)

page_content: - inform about offence to all concerned officers.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: in the instances mentioned above (a, b,& c)

page_content: - make endorsement on the complaint mentioning - date and time of receipt of complaint/statement. - the crime number. - section of law of ipc, special and local laws shall be applied as per the contents of the petition and nature of case. - for example - if it is of rioting and hurt case apply sections such as 147, 148, 149, 324, 325, 326 r/w 34 -191(2), 191(3),190,118,117(1), 3(5) bns. - if wrongful restriction is there section 341 ipc /127 bns is to be added - if wrongly confinement is there section 342 ipc/127 bns is to be added, - if wrongful tress pass is there section 447/329 bns is to be added - if house trespass is there sections 448/329 bns,452/333 bns are to be added - make an entry in the general diary. - register the case by issuing fir. - provide copy of fir free of cost to complainant (sec. 154 (2) cr.p.c./173 bnss) - dispatch original fir to jurisdictional magistrate forthwith. - write requisition to the medical officer for treatment, examination of injuries and for issuing medical certificate/ injuries certificate - write duty passport to constable for escorting the injured person to hospital. - if required, arrangements should be made for getting recording dying declaration by magistrate. - i.o. proceeds to scene of crime with io kit box and case file.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: soon after the receipt of the complaint, following things are to be done

page_content: - despatch two staff members to soc with required material of guarding scene (cones and scene cordoning tape/ribbon) to preserve the evidence. instruct them to control the curious on lookers who may disturb scene of offence and also clear unauthorised persons. - making arrangements for sending injured persons to hospital from the scene.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: investigation activity like survey and recording of scene of offence:

page_content: - note the time of arrival. - note weather conditions, especially at outdoor crime scenes. - note down the co-ordinates (latitudes-longitudes) of the scene of crime. - clear unauthorized persons from the scene. - prevent anyone disturbing anything in the entire scene of offence. - identifying and fixing the permanent objects/ reference points. - secure the presence two respectable and independent panchas (mediators) of the locality. - at night obtain adequate lighting before scene processing commences. artificial lighting used must be adequate for photography and for minute detail search for items such as hair, cartridge cases, etc. - reconstruction of scene with the help of eye witnesses. - enter scene by route least likely to disturb evidence, noting route of travel. - minute examination of scene of offence by following standard methods for identifying physical evidence/material objects in the presence of panchas. - provide numbers to each identified material object (m.o.) so that they are clearly visible in photographs and easy to explain about such m.os in panchanama and sketch map of scene of crime. - take measurements of the scene carefully with specific reference to permanent object/reference point, material objects and other important features of the crime. - take digital photographs of scene on following lines. - cover all the material objects and reference points (long shot) - cover all the material objects identified - each material object shall be photographed separately (close shots) - draw sketch map mentioning the measurements recorded. - collect material objects/physical evidence which are useful in the investigation like weapons of offence, blood stains and pack carefully without causing any damage. - the process of packing, labelling and sealing attestation by panch

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: investigation activity like survey and recording of scene of offence:

page_content: witnesses should be completed at the scene crime itself.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: investigation activity like survey and recording of scene of offence:

page_content: - prepare crime detail form (iif-ii). - panchanama (mediator report) shall be drafted by describing the complete details whatever is done at scene of offence in the presence of mediators by explain each and every aspect to them because they are the witnesses who speaks about entire process in the court. - the date and time of starting of panchanma and closing time of panchanama shall be mentioned in panchanama. - preparing panchanama and sketch map are useful for fixing the scene of offence, reconstruction of scene of offence. - after recording panchanama, the io shall read over the contents to panchas and obtain signatures when the panchas agree the contents as true.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: activity at hospital (medical officer examining the injured person)

page_content: - the injured to be got examined by medical officer. - receiving injury certificate from medical officer. - seizure of blood stained shirt of victim (if blood stains are there) at hospital after completion of examination of wounds by medical officer. - the process of packing, labelling and sealing attestation by panch witnesses should be completed at the hospital. - send the seized blood stained shirt/clothes to fsl as material evidence (blood stained shirt should be dried in the shade before packing) will help as material evidence.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: guidelines for recording statements of witnesses

page_content: - injuries mentioned in the medical certificates should tally with the injuries mentioned in the statements of witnesses (overt acts). - recording of statements of all connected witnesses u/s 161 cr.p.c. - injured/victims statements - eye witnesses - circumstantial witnesses - witnesses who speak about the motive, preparation, previous and subsequent conduct of accused - relevant u/s 8 of iea/6 bsa.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: activity at the place of arrest of accused

page_content: - i.o. reaches the house of accused.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: activity at the place of arrest of accused

page_content: - accused person found at residence. - accused arrested by observing the arrest formalities (secs. 41, 41- a, 41-b, 46, 50 cr.p.c./35(1)/(2),35(3)/(6),36,43,47 bnss). - intimate about the arrest, to the friend or relative nominated by the accused. serve a notice as per section 50-a cr.p.c /48 bnss to that effect. - prepare the arrest memo (iif-iii), take the signature of such nominated person on arrest memo. - record statement of accused to know various aspects of offence and his defence plea as a part of criminal justice system.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: after arrest - examination of accused by medical officer under section 53 cr.p.c/51 bnss

page_content: - prepare the requisition addressing the medical officer for examining the injured person. - provide treatment for the purpose of medical certificate. - receiving of medical certificate from medical officer. - if blood stains are found on the clothes of the accused, seize the same as per the procedure for sending the same to fsl.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: activity in police station immediately after returning from field

page_content: - process of entering the details of accused and material objects in the records of the police station. - prisoner search register. - sentry relief book. - general diary. - collect further evidence to rebut the defence of the accused. - collecting further evidence writing of remand report and sending the accused persons for judicial custody.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: further investigation till filing of charge sheet

page_content: - whenever investigation is taken up in the case, write case diary incorporating entire process of investigation done on that day (section 172 cr.p.c./192 bnss record of investigation). - send material objects to fsl and other experts along with a letter of advice. - receiving of expert reports like fsl report, medical certificate of injured person.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: further investigation till filing of charge sheet

page_content: - review of evidence by i.o. before finalising investigation of cases: - the io shall review his own investigation and critically analyse the efforts made and evidence collected to establish the case. ----the entire facts, evidence, documentary / medical evidence if any is to be consolidated. - prepare notice to the complainant and serve the same on the complainant under acknowledgement (173 (2) (ii) cr.p.c./193 bnss). - the charge sheet shall be based on the facts and evidence collected by io and it shall not contain any presumptions and assumptions. - after completion of the investigation charge sheet has to be prepared carefully by covering all points in chronological order. memo of evidence shall be prepared, which gives the list of witnesses and what each witness is likely to say. - charge sheet with all enclosures shall be filed in the court. - while filing charge sheet in court care to be taken to deposit all the material objects and property in the court. the copy of the charge sheet in full shape to be furnished to the prosecuting officer.

page_content: - whoever causes the death of any person - by doing any rash or negligent act - not amounting to culpable homicide - shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term, which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. - ingredients of rash or negligent act in the section:

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 304-a ipc/106 (1) bns causing death by negligence -

page_content: a rash act is primarily an overhasty act in rashness, the criminality lies in running the risk of doing an act with recklessness or indifference to consequences.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 304-a ipc/106 (1) bns causing death by negligence -

page_content: negligence means breach of duty caused by omission to do something which a reasonable man guided by those considerations which ordinarily regulate conduct of human affairs would do or doing something which a prudent or reasonable man would not do.

page_content: - when any person is injured or any property of a third party is damaged, - as a result of an accident in which a motor vehicle is involved, - the driver of the vehicle or other person in charge of the vehicle shall-

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 134 - duty of driver in case of accident and injury to a person:-

page_content: section 134(a) take all reasonable steps to secure medical attention for the injured person, and, if necessary, convey him to the nearest hospital, unless it is not practicable to do so on account of mob fury or any other reason beyond his control.

page_content: section 134(b) report the circumstances of the occurrence to the nearest police station, including the circumstances, if any, for not taking reasonable steps to secure medical attention as required under clause (a), as soon as possible, and in any case within twenty-four hours of the occurrence.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

page_content: section 187 punishment for offences relating to accident:- whoever fails to comply with the provisions of section 134 shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both or, if having been previously convicted of an offence under this section, he is again convicted of an offence under this section, with imprisonment for a term, which may extend to six months, or with fine, which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.

a road accident or traffic accident means an accident involving at least one vehicle on a road which is open to public traffic in which at least one person is injured or killed or vehicle collides with an other vehicle, pedestrian, animal, road debris or other stationary obstruction, such as a tree, pole or building. traffic collisions often result in injury, disability, death, and property damage as well as financial costs to both society and the individuals involved.

kidnapping means taking away a person against his/her will by force, threat or by deceitful means. usually, the purpose of kidnapping is to get a ransom, or for some political or other purposes etc.
kidnapping: kidnapping can be classified as 'kidnapping from india' or 'kidnapping from lawful guardianship'.
abduction: abduction means if a person either by force compels a person or induces another person to go from any place is said to abduct such person.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: present day scenario - kidnapping/abduction of persons

page_content: the reasons for kidnapping/abduction are numerous. the unscrupulous persons are resorting to kidnapping/abduction to settle their scores. the extremists' kidnappings are mostly for ransom, murder and to get their cadres released from jails. owing to tremendous increase in the value of the lands at all places, the land mafia is playing vital role in kidnapping / abduction for settlements and school going children are also being kidnapped.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: present day scenario - kidnapping/abduction of persons

page_content: one of the most important considerations for the police when investigating cases of kidnapping is providing support to the immediate family and close friends. in some cases, the trauma associated with such an event will place the family and friends of the kidnapped person under severe personal pressure, particularly if the investigation or the media make heavy demands on them.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: present day scenario - kidnapping/abduction of persons

page_content: the analysis of risk involved in a case of a kidnapped person is very vital. it may lead to kidnapping for ransom, settlements, murder, rape and murder, factionist issues, political issues, child abuse, sexual exploitation, domestic violence, human trafficking, forced marriage etc.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: present day scenario - kidnapping/abduction of persons

page_content: further the cause and effect of kidnapping needs to be given more weightage. the risk factor involved is very much essential to response to the issue. unless the risk factors involved are studied, a right direction and approach cannot be given to the investigation.

it can be committed only in respect of a minor or a person of unsound mind., abduction = it can be committed in respect of any person, male or female, minor or major and irrespective of his or her nationality.. the person kidnapped is removed out of lawful guardianship., abduction = there is no question of removal from lawful guardianship.. the minor or the person of unsound mind is simply taken away or enticed to go with the kidnapper., abduction = force, compulsion or deceitful means are used to convey him her from one place to other.. consent of person enticed is immaterial., abduction = consent of a person if freely and voluntarily given, no offence.. the intention of the offender is irrelevant., abduction = intention is an important factor.. punishable u/s 363 ipc., abduction = punishable u/s 364 ipc.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: if the sho receives information about the offence, while the accused are fleeing with kidnapped/ abducted victim

page_content: then the first priority of sho is to trace the accused and rescue the kidnapped victim by quickest means, by alerting patrol mobiles and by blocking roads and by organising nakabandi (a system of patrolling streets by use of check points) in the district and bordering unit and continuing such efforts till offenders are caught.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: if the sho receives information about the offence, while the accused are fleeing with kidnapped/ abducted victim

page_content: in such case sho shall entrust registering of a case about offence to his subordinate officer, who shall receive complaint/record statement about the offence from parents, blood relatives, friends of victim and will register case by issuing fir as per procedure by applying sections of law as per the contents of the petition/complaint

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: if the sho receives information about the offence, while the accused are fleeing with kidnapped/ abducted victim

page_content: - -provide copy of fir free of cost to complainant (sec. 154(2) - -cr. p.c. - -dispatch original fir to jurisdictional magistrate forthwith - -prepare plan of action for investigation and start investigation

- -to alert the concerned if a vehicle number is given. - -clearly examine the complainant eliciting following facts: - what is the age of the victim? - what is the background of the victim? - verify the antecedents of the victim - what is the demand from the kidnapper? - are the kidnappers known. - what is the mode of conveyance used by kidnappers? - how they kidnapped i.e., whether they came into house and picked the victim or picked on the way, or from school or college etc. - whether there is any acquaintance between victim and kidnapper. - -if the offence of kidnapping takes place in school, examine carefully the other children who might have noted down vehicle number. - -whether the victim or near and dear of victim are having enmity with anybody? - -develop portrait parley of accused with the help of eye witnesses who can provide information about descriptive particulars of accused persons.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: if the parents of victim receive phone call with some demand

page_content: - a) if the demand is over phone tracking of calls, call data, tower location are very important - b) form special teams - c) alert all patrol mobiles from time to time giving directions. - d) do not underestimate the offender. always be alert. offenders are always desperate. hence carry weapons loaded to meet exigencies. - e) it should be our main motto to safeguard the victim from the clutches of the kidnappers. hence, every care shall be taken in that angle. - f) at the same time repose confidence among family members of the victim and inform the efforts made, developments and progress made in the direction of tracing victim and arresting accused. - g) make all sincere and possible efforts including using information and communication technology call data record (cdr) analysis, cell tower data analysis and mapping, gps. - h) nab the culprits; recover the victim from the possession of the accused. - i) the io should collect sufficient evidence for establishing the case by examining eye witnesses, victim, circumstantial witnesses and their statements are to be reordered u/s 161 cr.p.c. - j) the statements of prime witnesses may be got recorded u/s 164 crpc/ 183 bnss.

- -carefully counsel the victim as victim is under shock. - -create confidence in the victim that he/she will be protected. - -record the statement of victim minutely depicting all information. - -total kidnapping period to be accounted for by examining owners of the stay where the victim was confined - -if the victim is woman, lady officer should counsel the victim and verify whether the victim was subjected to sexual assault. - -if necessary, subject her to medical examination. - -sometimes minors elope and her parents give kidnap contents. but on return minor simply states that she has gone somewhere. this aspect needs clear counter verification. - -victim to be produced in the court and handed over to the right person as per court orders.

metadata.heading: when culpable homicide is a murder?

page_content: - (1) if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing death, or- - (2) act done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows to be likely to cause the death of the person - (3) act done with the intention of causing bodily injury or inflicted injury is sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death- - (4) if the person committing knows that the act that is so imminently dangerous that it must, in all probability, cause death or such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, and commits such act without any excuse.

page_content: - visit the scene of offence; minutely examine the scene to preserve material objects. - take the photography and videography of the scene of offence. - conduct scene of offence panchanama and rough sketch before the mediators. - examine and record the statements of all relevant witnesses. - conduct inquest over the dead body. - depute search parties to apprehend the accused. - send the dead body to the hospital for pme. - s.h.o has to assess the retaliation from deceased party, if so safe guard the houses of suspected persons to avert further attacks.

page_content: - investigation activity like survey and recording of scene of offencenote the time of arrival. - note weather conditions, especially at outdoor crime scenes. - check perimeter of scene to ensure that entire scene is secured. note down the co-ordinates (latitudes-longitudes) of the scene of crime. - clear unauthorized person from the scene. - prevent anyone from touching the body or disturbing anything in the entire scene of offence. - identifying and fixing of the permanent objects/ reference points. secure the presence two respectable and independent panchas (mediators) of the locality. - do not question the victims hastily to know the details of offence- - allow the victims to stabilize. - ease tension of the victims by consoling them. - then examine them about the details of the offence. - at night -obtain adequate lighting before scene processing commences. artificial lighting used must be adequate for photography and for minute detail search for items such as hair, cartridge cases,

page_content: etc.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: investigation on reaching scene of offence

page_content: - when lighting not available - secure scene under guard and wait for daylight before processing is commenced. - reconstruction of scene with the help of eye witnesses. - place compass to know magnetic north. - enter scene by route least likely to disturb evidence, noting route of travel. - if scene of offence is very large organize scene search by adopting specific plan, assign tasks, areas of search to individual officers. minute examination of scene of offence by following standard methods for identifying physical evidence/material objects (m.os.) which are useful in the investigation like weapons of offense, blood stains etc. in the presence of panchas. - provide numbers to each identified material object (m.o.) so that they are clearly visible in photographs and easy to explain about in panchanma and sketch map of scene of crime. take measurements of the scene carefully with specific reference to permanent object/reference point, material objects and other important features of the crime. - make careful note of the following points in respect of dead body: - position of dead body. - physical condition of dead body and clothing. - location of any substances, injuries on deceased and his clothing. - ascertain any alteration of position of dead body after the offence before your arrival. - before removing dead body examine deceased for physical evidence for example: loose hairs, fibre, etc. - place deceased on a separate cloth sheet, move body shortest possible distance. - examine the ground underneath the victim. - examine deceased for additional physical evidence that may become visible after movement.

sec. 379 ipc/ sec 303 bns: punishment for theft - whoever commits theft shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
1. the accused removed the movable property; 2. he removed it out of the possession of another person without his consent; 3. he did so with a dishonest intention.

sec.454 ipc: lurking house-trespass or house-breaking in order to commit offence punishable with imprisonment -whoever commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, in order to the commit any offence punishable with imprisonment, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offence intended to be committed is theft, the term of the imprisonment may be extended to ten years.

1. all the ingredients required under the preceding sec. 453 ipc must be present. in addition to that, it is necessary to establish that the dominant intention of the entry was to cause annoyance, intimidation or insult . 2. joint responsibility- culpability is not excluded by mere distance of accused from scene of crime .

lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night in order to commit offence punishable with imprisonment .-whoever commits lurking house-trespass by night, or house-breaking by night, in order to the committing of any offence punishable with imprisonment, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offence intended to be committed is theft, the term of the imprisonment may be extended to fourteen years.

1. all the ingredients required under the preceding sec. 453 ipc must be present. in addition to that, it is necessary to establish that the dominant intention of the entry was to cause annoyance, intimidation or insult . 2. joint responsibility- culpability is not excluded by mere distance of accused from scene of crime .

sec.380 ipc/ (bns 305): theft in dwelling house, etc.whoever commits theft in any building, tent or vessel, which building, tent or vessel is used as a human dwelling, or used for the custody of property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

1. accused removed movable property; 2. he removed the property out of the possession of another person without his consent; 3. he did so with a dishonest intention; 4. the property was removed from the building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or for the custody of the property
sec. 384 ipc: punishment for extortion - whoever commits extortion shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

1. the accused puts a person in fear of injury to such person or any other person; 2. the putting of a person in such fear must be intentional; 3. the accused must thereby induce the person so put in fear to deliver to any person any property, valuable security or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security; 4. such inducement must be done dishonestly.
section 171b/171e:/ sec 170 & 173 bns bribery &punishment for bribery
who ever gives a gratification to any person to induce him to exercise any electoral right.
accepts for himself/some one else any gratification as a reward for exercising any such right, commits the offence of bribery

- (1) whoever voluntarily interferes or attempts to interfere - with the free exercise of any electoral right - commits the offence of 'undue influence' at an election. - (2) without prejudice to the generality of the provision of sub-section (1), - whoever- - (a) threatens any candidate or voter, or - any person in whom a candidate or voter is interested, - with injury of any kind, or - (b) induces or attempts to induce a candidate or voter - to believe that he or any person in whim he is interested will become or will be rendered an object of divine displeasure or of spiritual censure, - shall be deemed to interfere with the free exercise of the electoral right of such candidate or voter, within the meaning of sub- section (1). - (3) a declaration of public policy or - a promise of public action, or - the mere exercise of a legal right without intent to interfere with an electoral right, - shall not be deemed to be interference within the meaning of this section.

- whoever at an election applied for a voting paper or - votes in the name of any other person, whether living or dead, or in a fictitious name, or - who having voted once at such election applies at the same election for a voting paper in his own name , and

- whoever abets, procures or attempts to procure the voting by any person in any such way, commits the offence of 'personation at an election'. - provided that nothing in this section shall apply to a person - who has been authorized to vote as a proxy for an elector under any law for the time being in force in so far as he votes as proxy for such elector.

- whoever commits the offence of bribery - shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term - which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both . - provided that bribery by treating shall be punished with fine only . - explanation .-'treating' means that form of bribery - where the gratification consists in - food, - drink , - entertainment, or - provision.

- the offence is punishable with imprisonment for one year or fine or with both. - if it is with treating, fine only. - it is non-cognizable, bailable, non-compoundable and - triable by the magistrate of the first class.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 171-f/ sec. 174 bns. punishment for undue influence or personation at an election.-

page_content: - whoever commits the offence of undue influence or - personation at an election - shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term - which may extend to one year or with fine, or with both .
- the offence of undue influence is non-cognizable while personation is cognizable.
- both are bailable and non-compoundable and triable by a magistrate of the first class.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 171-g / sec 175 bns . false statement in connection with an election.-

page_content: - whoever with intent to affect the result of an election - makes or publishes any statement which is false or does not believe to be true , - in relation to the personal character or conduct of any candidate - shall be punished with fine .
- the offence is punishable with fine only. - it is non-cognizable, bailable, non-compoundable and - triable by magistrate of the first class.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: 171-h / sec 176 bns . illegal payments in connection with an election.-

page_content: - whoever without the general or special order in writing of candidate - incurs or authorizes expenses on account of the holding of any public meeting, or - upon any advertisement, circular or publication - in any other way whatsoever for the purpose of promoting or procuring the election of such candidate, - shall be punished with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees : - provided that if any person having incurred any such expenses - not exceeding the amount of ten rupees without authority - obtains within ten days from the date on which such expenses were incurred - the approval in writing of the candidate, - he shall be deemed to have incurred such expenses with the authority of the candidate.
- the offence is punishable with fine of rs.500/- only. - it is non-cognizable, bailable, non-compoundable and - triable by magistrate of the first class.

- article 324:- election commission - independent forum - conduct of elections to president, vice- president, parliament, state legislatures. - president will appoint chief election commissioner and other commissioners. - chief election commissioner will act as chairman. - president may appoint regional election commissioners if necessary. - tenure of office - as decided by president. - removal of chief election commissioner as per the procedure of supreme court judge. - removal of other commissioners on recommendation of chief election commissioner only. - president/governor shall make available the required staff. - article 325:- no discrimination for inclusion in electoral rolls. - article 326:- all citizens of 18 years & above are eligible except disqualified by state for non residence, unsoundness of mind, criminals etc., - art 327 & 328:- power of parliament and state legislature to make provisions for elections and preparation of rolls. etc., - art 329:- bar of jurisdiction by courts. - can challenged only by an election petition.
- bribery

professional ethics are rules of behaviour to which members of a profession subscribe. we, as police officers, should set ourselves and uphold such norms of behaviour as would bring us closer to the people. under the law, we are invested with extensive powers, powers affecting rights and liberties of people. unless we restrain ourselves in the exercise of authority and implement law with impartiality and fairness, the police will continue to, be estranged from the people. it is, therefore, important that young police officers should implement law in such a way that fairness and impartiality are the touch-stone of all law enforcement.

let us start with the registration of cases. under the law, it is obligatory for police officers to record every information relating to the commission of cognizable offence (154 crpc/ 173 bnss). if the information is given orally, it should be reduced to writing and recorded and a copy of it shall be given free of cost (sec.154 crpc/ 173 bnss) to the informant. three common charges leveled against police officers in this regard are:

- (a) complaints are not recorded at all - (b) complaints are not recorded when they relate to places outside the ps jurisdiction; - (c) copies of the complaints are not furnished to the informant in spite of the statutory obligation to do so.

a reading of section 154 crpc/ sec 173 bnss would convince you that it is obligatory for an officer-in-charge of a ps to record every information relating to the commission of cognizable offence. the section does not refer of jurisdiction at the stage of registration. you need jurisdiction to investigate but not to register a cognizable offence. also, instructions are very clear in the appm (order no.495) that even when a cognizable offence reported relates to a place outside the jurisdiction of a police station, the sho should record the complaint and then transfer fir to the police station having jurisdiction. avoiding registration would not only cause undeserved harassment to the complainant but would also delay the process of investigation. some time ago, two police stations in hyderabad squabbled over jurisdiction and delayed registration of a case of abduction. the delay was fatal for the victims who were done to death by the time the police could react to the situation.
in respect of offences against property, three common malpractices are:
- (a) starting with the premise that the complaint is false; - (b) under valuing the stolen property and overvaluing the recovered property and
- (c) minimizing the gravity of the offence.

an investigator should keep an open mind when he embarks on any investigation. if he starts even with the faintest suspicion that the case is false, the whole perspective would be distorted and it would be difficult to investigate the case effectively. it is common experience that people generally avoid the police. unless one is a hardened criminal or has a strong motive, one would not venture to prefer a complaint that is not true. in any case, it is wrong to refer a case as false unless the investigator can establish its falsity beyond doubt; mere surmise would not do. of the second malpractice, the less said the better.

wherever it happens, it happens with the connivance of supervisory officers. the victim of the offence will not only not think well of the police, he might even suspect them of planning to help themselves to the stolen property.

it is not uncommon that dacoities are registered as robberies and robberies as thefts. apart from being unethical, such practices would prevent the true picture of crime emerging. unless we record all crime sincerely and unless we have a good idea of the intensity of the problem, we cannot do anything to improve matters. for solving any problem we must first recognise it, then appreciate it and then plan effective preventive action. no problem is solved by refusal to take cognizance of it. whether you register a dacoity as robbery or theft, it is still a dacoity. it is this kind of malpractices that have eroded public confidence in the police.
it is important to know whether arrest and search are necessary at all and if so, at what stage of investigation.

powers governing search and arrest invested in police officers are extensive and should be used with great restraint. sec. 157 crpc/ 176 bnss clearly says that an i.o should 'proceed to the spot to investigate the facts and circumstances of the case and if necessary, to take measures for the discovery and arrest of the offender'. in other words, the law clearly lays down that the arrest should be resorted to only after proper investigation takes place. instead many police officers often rush with arrests soon after, those who are initially arrested for their alleged or suspected involvement may not be found guilty at the end of the investigation. while arrests may be necessary in cases where sensation or uproar caused by the occurrence of the offence warrants immediate arrest of the accused, in other cases it would be better to defer arrests till investigation takes shape and the involvement, of the alleged accused becomes certain. in a large -number of cases, arrests can wait till the investigations are completed. unfortunately, often, owing to many reasons including pressures from different quarters, subordinate police officers resort to arrests even before all the evidence is collected. as a result, in many cases, i.os would have arrested people who, at the end of the investigation, are found to be innocent or would have been important witnesses.

as arrest at the hands of the police nearly destroys the prestige of a person in society, it is most important that this power is exercised with great caution. it is no solace for a person if he is initially arrested and is declared innocent of the offence after the investigation is completed. in the process, he would have suffered all the ignominy and humiliation that go with arrest. similarly an i.o has to be very cautious in resorting to searches. a place or a house belonging to a person should not be searched as of routine. it should be clearly assessed whether a search is necessary at all or whether what is sought to be accomplished in the search can be otherwise achieved. like arrest, search by the police also and when a search takes place, the reputation of the person involved is severely affected.
informal arrests and searches are not only not contemplated in law but reprehensible and are largely the reason for public disfavour.

an investigating officer has a responsibility to ask the accused person if he has anything to say to prove his innocence. except in a few organizations like the acb, the investigating officers have given up this moral obligation cast on them to record the statement of the accused person. fairness would require that they invariably record the statement of the accused person unless he is unwilling to make any statement at the stage. that would, in fact, enable us to scrutinize the investigation in the light of the statement of the accused person. however, while doing so, we should not suppress any fact or circumstances that are favorable to the accused; if at all, we should re-examine his complicity in the light of his statement. if there is enough evidence to show that his assertions are wrong that would strengthen the prosecution case. if on verification, the investigating officer finds that his involvement is not there or not to the extent alleged, he has a moral responsibility to bring it on record.

the reputation of the police today is that they do not give a fair chance to the accused. they do not care to know his version and do nothing to help him if he is falsely implicated. as a result, a person who is falsely implicated in a case would rather obtain anticipatory bail from a court, than go to the investigating officer to seek redress. most people feel that the police arrest a person once his name is cited in the fir and would not heed any protestations of innocence. unless we live down this reputation, our image cannot improve.

a common complaint against the police is that they equate the aggressor and the victim in the investigation of rioting cases. when a large scale rioting has taken place between two groups and the rioting forms part of one transaction, it is the responsibility of the police officer to decide:

- (a) who has taken the initiative; and - (b) who is the aggressor. it is the aggressor who should be dealt with under the law: the victims cannot be found fault with for using force in defending themselves. the common practice, in all such situations is, that cases and counter cases are registered and both the groups are charge sheeted; that is, the complainant in one case becomes the accused in the other case and vice versa.

such a disposal is clear abdication of responsibility on the part of the police and their failure to investigate and establish the truth. no wonder in such cases, one group or the other feels that the investigating officer has been less than fair to them. in fact, charge sheeting the victims of aggression for defending themselves which they have every right to do, is adding insult to injury.
similarly while proceeding against factious groups u/s 107 crpc/ 126 bnss, information is filed in a routine manner against both the contending groups.

in fact, many police officers believe that it is obligatory to proceed against both the factions uls.107 crpc. this is not correct but such a course of action would leave a trail of bitterness against the police. a responsible police officer should strive to know who the aggressors are and proceed against them u/s.107 crpc. there is no harm in laying information against one group or individual if the police officer is convinced that the group or individual is given to aggressive behavior and it is perfectly legal.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: laying charge sheet in the absence of convincing evidence:

page_content: you would very often come across cases where you are your-self not convinced of the type and quantum of evidence available against a person or persons. in such cases, a responsible police officer should satisfy himself that the evidence is reliable and adequate. instead, many officers shirk this responsibility and chargesheet the cases, in a routine way taking satisfaction from the fact that he is 'leaving it to the court'. this happens even in cases where investigating officers have reasons to suspect that the evidence against a person is trumped up. it is the responsibility of police officer first to decide whether there is enough evidence against the accused to take him to a court of law and whether that evidence is genuine. when you are convinced that the evidence is not adequate or is trumped up, it is unethical to send up a person for trial. knowing well that the case may not stand the
